front: What is the Union of two graphs G1 and G2?
back: The union of two graphs G1 = (V1, E1) and G2 = (V2, E2) is a graph G = (V1 ∪ V2, E1 ∪ E2). It contains all vertices and edges from both graphs. If both graphs have the same vertices, it's called vertex union; otherwise it's the disjoint union.

front: Define the Intersection of two graphs
back: The intersection G1 ∩ G2 contains only the vertices and edges that are common to both graphs. It requires both graphs to share the same vertex set. Formally, G1 ∩ G2 = (V1 ∩ V2, E1 ∩ E2).

front: What is the Join operation in graph theory?
back: The join G1 + G2 (or G1 ∨ G2) combines two vertex-disjoint graphs by connecting every vertex of G1 to every vertex of G2. The resulting graph has V(G1) ∪ V(G2) vertices and E(G1) ∪ E(G2) ∪ {uv | u∈V(G1), v∈V(G2)} edges.

front: Define the Complement of a graph G
back: The complement G̅ of a simple graph G has the same vertex set as G, but two vertices are adjacent in G̅ if and only if they are NOT adjacent in G. Formally, u-v ∈ E(G̅) iff u-v ∉ E(G).
A subgraph H = (V', E') of G = (V, E) is a graph where V' ⊆ V and E' ⊆ E, with each edge in E' having both endpoints in V'. An induced subgraph contains all edges between vertices in V'.

front: What is the Cartesian Product of two graphs?
back: The Cartesian product G □ H has vertex set V(G) × V(H). Two vertices (u1,v1) and (u2,v2) are adjacent if either u1=u2 and v1v2∈E(H), or v1=v2 and u1u2∈E(G). Example: Grid graphs are Cartesian products of paths.

front: Define the Tensor (Direct) Product of graphs
back: The tensor product G × H has vertex set V(G) × V(H). Vertices (u1,v1) and (u2,v2) are adjacent if u1u2∈E(G) AND v1v2∈E(H). Also called categorical product or Kronecker product.

front: What is the Strong Product of graphs?
back: The strong product G ⊠ H combines properties of Cartesian and tensor products. Vertices (u1,v1) and (u2,v2) are adjacent if either they are adjacent in Cartesian product OR they are adjacent in tensor product (or both).

front: Define the Lexicographic Product of graphs
back: The lexicographic product G[H] has vertices as pairs (u,v) where u∈V(G), v∈V(H). Vertices (u1,v1) and (u2,v2) are adjacent if either u1u2∈E(G), or u1=u2 and v1v2∈E(H). It's also called composition.

Two graphs G and H are isomorphic if there exists a bijection f: V(G) → V(H) such that uv ∈ E(G) iff f(u)f(v) ∈ E(H). The mapping f is called an isomorphism. Isomorphic graphs have the same structure but may look different.
A spanning subgraph of G is a subgraph that contains all vertices of G but possibly missing some edges. It's used in network design and includes spanning trees as special cases.
A graph H is a minor of G if H can be obtained from G by repeatedly contracting edges, deleting edges, and deleting vertices. Graph minor theory is fundamental in the proof of the Four Color Theorem.
Edge contraction removes an edge uv from a graph and merges its two endpoints u and v into a single vertex. All edges that were incident to u or v now become incident to the new merged vertex.
The Ringel-Kötgen theorem states that every complete graph K_n can be decomposed into (n-1) copies of any tree with n vertices. This is a fundamental result in graph decomposition theory.
A graph decomposition is a partition of the edge set into edge-disjoint subgraphs. Common decompositions include edge-coloring (into matchings) and tree decomposition (into paths/trees).
